headline: China's Great Wall of Green Reclaims Desert After Decades of Work
meta_description: Half a century of effort using straw checkerboards has shrunk desertified land in northern China significantly since 2000.
summary_short: Northern China's massive reforestation program has reversed decades of sand expansion, transforming 200,000 square miles with forests.
body:
Across northern China, a quiet transformation is underway. For the past fifty years, workers have deployed an elegantly simple technique: laying straw in checkerboard patterns across sand dunes. These humble barriers stabilize the shifting sands and create pockets where plants can finally take hold.

The strategy is part of the Three-North Protective Forest Program, also known as the Green Great Wall. Since 2000, the results have been striking. Desertified land in the region has shrunk significantly, with forests now covering 200,000 square miles of previously barren terrain.

What makes this achievement remarkable is its scale of human involvement. The initiative has engaged over 300 million rural laborers, mostly working part-time while balancing other responsibilities. These aren't remote corporate efforts—they represent sustained community commitment across generations.

Experts emphasize that maintaining these gains requires more than past dedication. Long-term commitment and continued community involvement remain essential for the program's success.

The straw checkerboards represent something powerful: proof that environmental damage, even when entrenched over decades, need not be permanent. With patience, technique, and collective effort, landscapes can heal.
